hook: You already know that linear functions create straight lines, but the real world rarely moves in straight lines — projectiles arc, profits curve, and structures bend. Quadratic functions are the mathematical language behind all of these shapes, and right now you're missing the tools to read and write that language fluently. By the end of this unit, you'll be able to look at a parabola and instantly understand its story.
concept: Every topic in this unit connects to one big idea: the parabola is a powerful, flexible curve that can be written, transformed, solved, and applied in multiple ways. Formally, we study the quadratic function $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$ and its many equivalent forms.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to graph $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$ by identifying the vertex, axis of symmetry, and intercepts directly from standard form.
Master graphing $f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k$ by reading the vertex $(h, k)$ and direction of opening at a glance.
Apply shifts, reflections, and stretches to the parent function $f(x) = x^2$ to predict exactly how any change in the equation reshapes the parabola.
You will sharpen your factoring skills — including trinomials and special cases — so you can efficiently find the zeros of any quadratic expression.
You will solve inequalities like $ax^2 + bx + c > 0$ by combining your graphing and factoring skills to identify which $x$-values satisfy the condition.
Apply everything you've learned to build quadratic models for real-world situations — from projectile motion to maximum profit — and interpret your solutions in context.
